title: Chapter 17: Human Again
content: Cold.

That's the first thing. Not the good cold — not the brisk, wakeful, orange-juice cold of a morning that promises to be fine. This is the wrong cold. The wet cold. The cold that lives in the spaces where fur used to be.
Chloe opens her eyes.

The ceiling is wrong. It's too close. No — she's too far from it. The beanbag is beneath her but it doesn't fit, she doesn't fit, her body is the wrong shape and the wrong size and there are *angles* where there should be curves and her spine is a chain of small disasters stacked vertically and something is pressing against her lower back and she can feel *it* — every individual vertebra, every disc, every muscle fiber screaming like they've been waiting for this moment to collect on a debt she didn't know she owed.
She sits up.

The world reorients. Two legs. Two legs unfold from the beanbag like an anatomy diagram she doesn't want to study. Knees. Ankles. *Toes.* She has toes again. Ten of them, pale and knobby on the cold floor, and she can feel every single one individually and that's the problem, that's the whole problem — she can feel *everything.* The air on her skin. The fabric of the t-shirt against her shoulders. The hair falling past her ears. The *ears*, small and flat and useless, pressed against her skull like an afterthought.
She stands.

Vertigo. The room tilts. She's too high up. The perspective is wrong — she can see the top of the kitchen counter, the top of the refrigerator, the top of Maya's easel. She hasn't been this tall in months. Her head is a balloon tethered to a body that feels borrowed, rental, someone else's suit that doesn't quite fit at the shoulders.

Her hands. She raises them. Ten fingers. Knuckles. Cuticles. The tiny scar on her left index finger from a paper cut in 2019. She remembers the paper cut. She remembers the meeting she was in when she got it. Quarterly review. Debra Wu's voice saying "Let's circle back to the KPIs" and the sting of the paper and the realization that this was her life now, these were her hands, this was what they did — shuffled documents and typed reports and held phones during meetings that could have been emails.
Her chest tightens.
No. Not yet. Not now.

She finds her phone on the floor beside the beanbag. Dead. Black screen. She plugs it in and waits, and the waiting is the worst part because the silence fills up with thoughts, actual thoughts, not the pleasant ambient fuzz of capybara consciousness — a warm hum of sun-water-food-safe — but *human thoughts*, which are loud and sharp and layered and every single one of them has an opinion.
The phone vibrates. Once. Twice. Then the notifications start coming like rain, like hail, like someone turned on a fire hose aimed directly at her face.

Slack. Email. LinkedIn. Voicemail. Slack. Email. Email. Email. A text from a number she doesn't recognize, probably work, probably someone who needs something, probably someone who has been waiting for her to come back and is *not happy about the wait.* Email. Slack. Email. A voicemail from Debra Wu's assistant — not Debra herself, never Debra herself, always the assistant, because Chloe wasn't important enough for direct access to the person making her miserable.
Her thumb hovers over the Slack icon.

She can fix this. That's what her brain says. You have hands. You can type. You can call. You can explain. You can negotiate. You can go back to being the person who handles things, who pushes through, who shows up even when showing up feels like swallowing glass. You can *fix this.*
Her thumb is shaking. She can see it trembling over the icon, this small bone-and-skin thing that she hasn't used in months, that used to type 80 words per minute and now can barely hold still.

She walks to the kitchen. Her feet know the way — muscle memory, human memory, the body recording every midnight snack and morning coffee and three-AM glass of water, storing the route in some deep place that capybara-Chloe never accessed because capybara-Chloe didn't walk to the kitchen, capybara-Chloe waddled, and the route was different from six inches lower, and the counter was an obstacle, not a surface.

Maya is at the kitchen table. A canvas in front of her, a brush in her hand, headphones on, painting in that absorbed way that means she hasn't noticed anything outside a two-foot radius for at least an hour.
She looks up.
Their eyes meet.

Maya takes the headphones off. Slowly. Her face does something complicated — a whole symphony of expressions in the space of a second. Recognition. Shock. Concern. Something that looks almost like grief.
"Chloe?"
"I'm back."
Maya sets the brush down. Its tip touches the canvas and leaves a small mark that neither of them notices. "Are you?"
The question lands. Not soft. Not hard. Just — precise. Maya-precise, the kind of question that sounds simple but isn't, that contains seventeen sub-questions, all of them important.
"I have hands." Chloe holds them up. Demonstrates. Fingers spread. "I can type. I can call Debra Wu. I can fix the — the resignation, the job, all of it. I can go back."
"Is that what you want?"
The question hangs between them like a held breath.
Chloe opens her mouth. Closes it. Opens it again.

What she wants. What an interesting concept. What a *luxurious* concept. When did she last want something that wasn't food or warmth or the particular pleasure of being exactly where she was? When did she last want something that required choosing?
She wants to not be asked this question.

She sits at the kitchen table. Maya slides the laptop toward her — *her* laptop, the one that's been closed for months, the one that still has the dent in the corner from when she dropped it rushing to a meeting she was already late for because the F train was delayed and Debra Wu didn't care about the F train, Debra Wu cared about deliverables, and Chloe had learned to care about deliverables too, had learned to compress her entire self into someone who produced output on schedule.
She opens it. The screen fills with light.
Email.
She starts reading. It seems like the thing to do. The productive thing. The human thing. The thing a person with hands and a laptop and 847 notifications would do.
The first email is from Debra Wu. *Chloe — we need to discuss your absence. Please reach out ASAP to discuss transition planning.* A dart of anxiety. Small. Manageable. She keeps reading.

The second email is from HR. *This is a reminder that your benefits enrollment period closes — * A larger dart. Benefits. Insurance. The scaffolding of adult life that she has been ignoring for as long as this capybara thing has been happening, which is apparently too long because the enrollment period is *closing* and she didn't know and she should have known and —

Third email. Debra again. *Chloe, this is my second email. I assume you're receiving these. Your absence is causing significant disruption.* Dart. Bigger. The word *disruption* doing its usual work, the way it always does, the way it was designed to — making her feel like the problem, like the thing that needs to be solved, like she is an inconvenience that other people are graciously tolerating.
Fourth. Fifth. Sixth. Debra. HR. A client she doesn't remember. Debra again. *Third email.* HR again. Something about tax documents. Debra. Debra. Debra.

By the tenth email, her chest is tight. Not metaphorically — physically. A band around her ribs, contracting, a fist closing around her lungs. She knows this feeling. She knows it like she knows her own name, which she hasn't used in months, which feels strange in her mouth now, like a word from a foreign language she used to speak.

Twentieth email. She can't breathe. Not fully. Each inhale is a negotiation, a transaction — partial air for partial effort, the body rationing oxygen like it's preparing for a shortage. Her fingers have stopped scrolling. She's staring at the screen but not reading. The words are shapes. The shapes are walls. The walls are closing.
Twenty-fifth email.
It hits.

The panic attack arrives like a train — no, like a *collapse*, like the floor has given way and she's falling through it, through the apartment, through the building, through the earth, and there is no bottom, just the falling, just the gasping, just the animal terror of a body that has decided it is dying and is acting accordingly.

Her hands grip the edge of the table. White knuckles. Her vision narrows — the laptop screen, bright and accusing, and then just the brightness, formless, blinding. Her heart is not beating, it is *hammering*, it is trying to crack her sternum from the inside, and she can't catch her breath and she can't slow down and she can't —
"Breathe."
Maya's voice. Far away. Close. Both.
"Breathe with me. In. Two three four. Out. Two three four."
She can't. Her lungs won't. The instructions are arriving in a language her body has forgotten, if it ever knew it, if it was ever something she could do on command.
"In. Two three four. You're safe. Out. Two three four. You're here."

She's not safe. She's not here. She's everywhere — she's in every email, every Slack message, every missed deadline and unanswered call and disappointed expectation. She is the sum of everything she hasn't done and the weight is crushing her and the room is spinning and —
"Breathe, Chloe. In. Two three four. That's it. Out. Two three four."

It takes twenty minutes. Twenty minutes of Maya's voice and Maya's hand on her back and Maya counting like counting is a form of love, like numbers can build a bridge back to the present moment. And slowly — so slowly, so painstakingly you could cry just watching it — Chloe's breath returns. Not all the way. Not to normal. But enough. Enough that she can feel the chair beneath her, the table under her hands, the air entering her body in something that approximates a rhythm.
The laptop screen has gone dark. The email inbox is hidden, temporarily, the benevolent conspiracy of a screen saver.

Chloe is shaking. Not the dramatic shaking of movies. The small, persistent shaking of a body that has been through something and hasn't fully accepted that it's over. Her hands. Her jaw. Her whole self, vibrating at a frequency just below visible.
"Hey," Maya says. Soft. Present.

The front door opens. Elif. Coming off a shift. Still in her scrubs, the new ones, the fancy ones, the ones she'd been so happy about eight hours ago. She sees them at the kitchen table and something in her face shifts — the nurse activating, the triage assessment, the person who knows what a panic attack looks like because she's seen a hundred of them in a different context.
She doesn't rush. She doesn't panic. She puts her bag down, takes off her shoes, and walks over. Sits in the third chair. Takes Chloe's hand.
"Hey," she says. "I'm here."
Chloe nods. Can't speak. The words are there but the path from brain to mouth is closed, under construction, something.

Elif starts counting. Different numbers than Maya — respiratory rate, heart rate, the clinical assessment disguised as conversation. But her voice is warm. Her hand is warm. Her scrubs smell like the hospital — antiseptic and something floral, the soap they use in the gift shop.
Minutes pass. Chloe's grip on Elif's hand loosens. Tightens. Loosens again.
"I don't want to be human," she says, and her voice is so small it barely makes it across the table, "if this is what human feels like."
The words land.

Maya and Elif look at each other over Chloe's head. Something passes between them — not words, not exactly, but the kind of communication that only happens between people who have shared a bathroom and a kitchen and an impossible situation and a capybara who was once a person and might be a person again and is currently neither and both.
They don't say *it's okay* or *it'll be fine* or any of the other things people say when they don't know what to say.
They just stay.
No satisfaction. None. This is the bottom. The lowest point on the graph, the deepest part of the well, the place where all the light has to travel the farthest to reach.
Capybara peace was real. Human suffering is real. The choice between them is clear and it is TERRIBLE.

Chloe sits at the kitchen table in her too-big t-shirt with her borrowed body and her shaking hands and her inbox full of everything she ever ran away from, and she is — for the first time since the transformation — completely, devastatingly, exactly who she has always been.
A person who is not okay.
A person who is still here.
